gm.name: John Schneider
gm.title: President of Football Operations / General Manager
gm.previous_role: Director of Football Operations, Green Bay Packers

career_path.role: Personnel Analyst to the General Manager
President of Football Operations / General Manager

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: Ron Wolf (Green Bay Packers, Hall of Fame GM) and Ted Thompson (Green Bay Packers GM)
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Ron Wolf tree → Ted Thompson extension. Wolf's influence shows in aggressive transaction volume, willingness to make bold trades, and building through all avenues. Thompson's influence shows in draft-centric roster building, patience in development, and valuing character. Schneider also spent time under Marty Schottenheimer in KC and Washington, which contributed a physical, tough identity to his roster preferences.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: Schneider diverged from Thompson's extreme conservatism in free agency — while Thompson was famously tight-fisted, Schneider has been more willing to sign veteran free agents (DeMarcus Lawrence, Sam Darnold, Cooper Kupp in 2025). Also diverged on transaction volume — Thompson was measured while Schneider has averaged more than 250 roster transactions per year. Recently adopted Ravens-style compensatory pick accumulation strategy after hiring Mike Macdonald from Baltimore.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: 16 drafts of independent data virtually eliminates need for lineage inference. Schneider IS the tree now — multiple current GMs (Scott Fitterer, Trent Kirchner candidates) came up under him.

key_influencers.name: Mike Macdonald
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Defensive-minded, Baltimore Ravens coaching tree. Values versatile defensive players who can play multiple positions. Scheme demands disruptive interior DL, press-capable CBs, and hybrid safeties. Prefers four-man pressure over blitzing. Offensive philosophy favors outside-zone run scheme.

key_influencers.name: Nolan Teasley
key_influencers.title: Assistant General Manager
key_influencers.known_biases: Homegrown Schneider protege — joined as scouting intern in 2013, rose through pro personnel ranks. Oversees football operations collaboratively with Schneider on all player acquisition. Has been considered for GM jobs elsewhere.

key_influencers.name: Matt Berry
key_influencers.title: Vice President / Player Acquisition
key_influencers.known_biases: Former director of college scouting (2015-2022), joined as southwest regional scout in 2008. Deep institutional knowledge of Schneider's draft board construction.

key_influencers.name: Trent Kirchner
key_influencers.title: Vice President of Player Personnel
key_influencers.known_biases: Being mentioned as GM candidate for other teams. Involved in cross-department player evaluation.

key_influencers.name: Jody Allen
key_influencers.title: Chair / Owner
key_influencers.known_biases: Schneider has stated she is 'thoughtful, engaging, and willing to make tough decisions.' Was involved in the decision to trade DK Metcalf and move on from Pete Carroll. Generally supports front office but engages on major roster decisions. Paul G. Allen Estate commenced formal team sale process in 2025 — ownership transition may affect dynamics.

front_office.power_dynamics: Schneider has full authority over the roster and draft. Under Pete Carroll (2010-2023), the relationship was described as 'Schneider sets the menu and Carroll picks specific players off of it.' Since Carroll's departure and Macdonald's hire in January 2024, Schneider holds clearly more power — he was the one who led the HC search and hired Macdonald. Macdonald has significant input on defensive personnel specifically, given his scheme demands. Schneider added the President of Football Operations title in January 2024, formally consolidating authority.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Build through the draft while making strategic acquisitions via trade and free agency. Schneider's official bio emphasizes 'building through the draft' and 'strategic acquisitions.'
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: Schneider is a moderate BPA drafter who tilts toward need more than he admits. In 2024, he took DT Byron Murphy II at 16 despite guard being the biggest need — but Murphy was widely considered the best defensive player in the draft and addressed the 31st-ranked run defense. In 2025, he took IOL Grey Zabel at 18 to directly address the biggest need. He consistently fills positional holes in rounds 2-4. Schneider also values athletic upside heavily — he has repeatedly drafted elite athletes with raw skill sets (DK Metcalf, Tariq Woolen, Nick Emmanwori) and developed them.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Claims BPA but has consistently addressed pressing needs in round 1 when the value aligns. The 2022 draft (Cross at OT, Mafe at EDGE) directly addressed post-Wilson holes. 2023 saw him take the top-rated CB at 5 and a WR at 20 to replace aging starters. 2024 was DT to fix run defense. 2025 was IOL to fix interior OL. The pattern is clear: Schneider takes BPA only when BPA and need overlap, and he engineers that overlap by trading actively.

trade_triggers: Clear tier break on board where desired player is available but at risk of being taken
Trading down when his board suggests remaining players are similar and he can accumulate Day 2 picks
Trading up on Day 2 or 3 for a specific player he covets (traded up for DK Metcalf, Tyler Lockett, Nick Emmanwori)
Trading veterans for draft capital (Russell Wilson, Geno Smith, DK Metcalf all traded for picks)
Accumulating Day 2 capital — has consistently valued picks in the 35-100 range
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Schneider is one of the most prolific draft-day traders in NFL history. PFF ranked him second among GMs in average value gained per draft from 2017-2021. He historically traded down in round 1 in six consecutive drafts (2013-2019), but has stayed put at his pick in the top-10/top-20 in recent years (2020 at 27, 2022 at 9, 2023 at 5 and 20, 2024 at 16, 2025 at 18). He values current-year Day 2 picks over future picks, and will trade future mid-rounders to get current-year picks. Recently began playing the compensatory pick game (Ravens influence via Macdonald hire).

position_avoidance: QB in early rounds — has drafted only Russell Wilson (3rd round, 2012), Alex McGough (7th, 2018), and Jalen Milroe (3rd, 2025) across 16 drafts. Prefers acquiring QBs via trade/FA rather than early-round draft capital.
IOL in round 1 — Grey Zabel in 2025 at pick 18 was his FIRST round-1 IOL selection in 16 drafts. Historically addressed interior OL in round 3 or later.
TE before round 4 — Elijah Arroyo at pick 50 in 2025 was his highest TE pick ever. Previously drafted TEs only on Day 3.
position_premium: EDGE — has drafted Bruce Irvin (Rd 1), Frank Clark (Rd 2), Darrell Taylor (Rd 2), Boye Mafe (Rd 2), Derick Hall (Rd 2), and L.J. Collier (Rd 1) at the position. Consistently invests early capital here.
CB — took Devon Witherspoon at 5 overall (2023), Tariq Woolen in Rd 5 (2022), Coby Bryant Rd 4 (2022), plus multiple late-round CBs. Heavy investment across all rounds.
WR — has drafted more WRs than any other position (16+), including DK Metcalf (Rd 2), Jaxon Smith-Njigba (Rd 1), Golden Tate (Rd 2), Tyler Lockett (Rd 3), Paul Richardson (Rd 2).
OT — Charles Cross (Rd 1), Russell Okung (Rd 1), Abraham Lucas (Rd 3) — consistently addresses tackle.

EDGE.archetype_summary: Explosive, high-motor edge rusher who can contribute in run defense. Schneider has drafted a mix of speed rushers and power-to-speed converters, but the consistent thread is first-step explosion and motor. Recent picks under Macdonald skew toward versatile stand-up/hand-in-dirt types who can fit a multiple front. Think Boye Mafe profile — explosive athlete who develops technique over time — rather than polished technician.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 32 inches — no clear hard cutoff but leans long
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Values first-step explosion. Vertical jump and broad jump matter as proxies for explosiveness. Speed (sub-4.65 40) strongly preferred.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.65 or faster preferred. Boye Mafe ran 4.53, Darrell Taylor 4.57, Bruce Irvin 4.48. L.J. Collier's 4.91 was the outlier and a bust.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: First-step explosion and closing speed — Schneider's best EDGE picks (Frank Clark, Mafe, Irvin) all won with burst off the line. Under Macdonald's scheme, versatility to rush from multiple alignments is now critical.
most_valued_traits: First-step explosion/get-off
High motor and relentless effort
Versatility to play multiple fronts (stand-up and hand-in-dirt)
Run defense discipline and setting the edge
Speed-to-power conversion
least_important_traits: Polished pass rush repertoire — has drafted raw athletes and developed them (Mafe, Irvin had limited moves entering NFL)
deal_breakers: Stiff athletes with limited burst — Collier's 4.91 was the exception and it was a disastrous pick
One-dimensional pass-rush-only players who can't defend the run
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite burst and closing speed — traded up for Irvin, took Mafe and Taylor in round 2 despite questions about technique

college_production.production_floor: No strict sack threshold — Mafe had 7 sacks as a senior, Irvin had 8, Taylor had 8.5. Collier had 6.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending production — Mafe broke out as a senior (7 sacks after modest prior years). Taylor was a late breakout.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — values production but will bet on traits when the athletic profile is exceptional

examples: 2012 1.15 — Bruce Irvin: 6-3, 245, 4.48 40. Speed rusher with elite get-off. Raw but explosive.
2015 2.63 — Frank Clark: 6-3, 271, 4.79 40. Power rusher with closing speed. 9.5 sacks as senior.
2020 2.48 — Darrell Taylor: 6-4, 255, 4.57 40. Explosive but raw, bendable edge. Developmental.
2022 2.40 — Boye Mafe: 6-4, 261, 4.53 40. Explosive speed-to-power EDGE from Minnesota. Raw but elite athletic profile.
2023 2.37 — Derick Hall: 6-3, 254, 4.51 40. Auburn EDGE with burst and motor. Productive senior year (8.5 sacks).
2019 1.29 — L.J. Collier: 6-3, 283, 4.91 40. Power rusher with poor speed. BUST — represents a clear departure from the archetype that failed.

OT.archetype_summary: Pass-protection-first tackle with athletic ability to operate in zone-blocking schemes. Schneider has consistently drafted tackles who are technically sound in pass protection over mauling run blockers. Charles Cross (2022) epitomizes the prototype — a fluid, agile pass protector from Mike Leach's Air Raid offense who has developed into a franchise LT. Under the new outside-zone scheme, lateral agility and ability to reach-block are even more important.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33.5 inches — Cross had 34+ inch arms, Okung had 34+, Lucas had 33.5. Long arms are non-negotiable at tackle.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Values foot quickness and lateral agility. Not a 40-time position but wants nimble feet for pass sets. Michael Jerrell (D-II pick) ran a 4.94 40 which is elite for OT.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-5.10 preferred — values movement skills over straight-line speed

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Pass protection technique and foot quickness. Cross was selected 9th overall primarily for his pass-blocking prowess in Mississippi State's pass-heavy offense.
most_valued_traits: Pass protection technique and mirror ability
Lateral agility for zone-scheme reach blocks
Length to keep rushers at bay
Durability and multi-year starting experience
Football IQ and ability to handle stunts/twists
least_important_traits: Dominant run blocking power — can be developed; Schneider prioritizes pass-pro first
deal_breakers: Short arms — will not draft a tackle with sub-33 inch arms
Stiff hips that can't redirect against speed rushers
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite foot quickness and mirror ability in pass protection

college_production.production_floor: Minimum two-year starter preferred. Cross started 2 years, Okung was a 4-year starter, Lucas was a multi-year starter.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values proven multi-year starters over one-year wonders
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Cross came from an Air Raid offense (pass-heavy), which Schneider recognized as a premium pass-pro proving ground

examples: 2010 1.6 — Russell Okung: 6-5, 310, 34.75-inch arms. Oklahoma State LT, multi-year starter. Pass-pro technician.
2022 1.9 — Charles Cross: 6-5, 307, 34.25-inch arms. Mississippi State LT, pass-protection specialist from Air Raid offense.
2022 3.72 — Abraham Lucas: 6-6, 315, 33.5-inch arms. Washington State RT, another Air Raid pass-pro tackle.
2021 6.208 — Stone Forsythe: 6-8, 310. Florida OT, massive frame with developmental upside.
2024 6.207 — Michael Jerrell: 6-5, 303. D-II Findlay OT, ran 4.94 40. Athletic development project.

IOL.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile interior lineman who fits an outside-zone scheme. Schneider historically underinvested in IOL (never took one in Rd 1 until 2025), but the Zabel pick at 18 overall signals a major shift. He values athleticism and movement ability over raw power — Zabel posted the best vertical jump among OL at the combine (36.5 inches), and Christian Haynes was lauded for his athletic profile. Versatility to play multiple spots is strongly preferred.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum for guards — Zabel has 32-inch arms (short for a lineman, but acceptable inside). Haynes also has adequate but not elite length.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Vertical jump and broad jump as explosiveness proxies. Zabel's 36.5-inch vertical was a key selling point. Shuttle and 3-cone for lateral agility. Schneider wants guards who can pull and reach-block in zone schemes.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No strict 40 time requirement, but movement skills matter. Values OL who move like tight ends.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Athletic ability to execute zone-scheme reach blocks and pulls. Must be able to get to the second level and block in space.
most_valued_traits: Athleticism and movement skills in space
Versatility to play multiple OL positions
Zone-blocking technique (reach blocks, combo blocks)
Competitive toughness and nastiness
Football IQ and communication skills
least_important_traits: Pure anchor strength in phone-booth pass protection — not the scheme requirement in an outside-zone system
deal_breakers: Stiff, non-athletic linemen who can't pull or reach block
Players who can only play one position on the interior
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athletic testing combined with versatility — Zabel was the top-rated practice player at the Senior Bowl

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter preferred. Zabel had 41 starts across 4 positions. Haynes was a 4-year starter.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values durability and longevity — every IOL drafted had extensive starting experience
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — values scheme fit (zone schemes) over raw production metrics

examples: 2025 1.18 — Grey Zabel: 6-6, 312, 32-inch arms, 36.5-inch vertical. NDSU OL, played all 5 positions. Zone-fit athletic marvel.
2024 3.81 — Christian Haynes: 6-3, 313. UConn RG, 4-year starter. Athletic guard who thrives in wide zone (89.2 PFF run grade in 2023).
2020 3.69 — Damien Lewis: 6-2, 327. LSU guard, physical run blocker from championship team.
2025 6.192 — Bryce Cabeldue: 6-4, 308. Kansas OT-to-guard convert. Athletic traits profile.
2025 7.234 — Mason Richman: Iowa guard. Physical zone-scheme blocker from power program.

DT.archetype_summary: Disruptive interior pass rusher who can also stuff the run. Schneider's DT investments accelerated dramatically with the Macdonald hire. Byron Murphy II (2024, pick 16) is the prototype — a penetrating 3-technique with elite burst, power, and the ability to play nose. Under Macdonald, the front four must dominate without blitzing (Seattle has one of the lowest blitz rates in the NFL). The DT must be a force multiplier who collapses the pocket from inside.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: First-step quickness and explosion off the ball are paramount. Murphy II ran a 4.87 40 at 297 lbs, which was elite for his position. Vertical and broad jump for explosiveness.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-5.00 40 preferred for 3-technique types. Two-gap nose tackles have more flexibility.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Interior pass rush disruption through first-step quickness and power. Murphy II's 12.5% pressure rate ranked 3rd among DTs (2021-2023). Must also be stout against the run — Seattle runs the #1 run defense under Macdonald.
most_valued_traits: First-step explosion and quickness off the snap
Interior pass rush disruption and pocket collapse
Run defense strength and anchor
Alignment versatility (3-tech and nose)
Motor and effort on every play
least_important_traits: Elite pass rush finesse moves — power and quickness are the priority over counter moves
deal_breakers: One-dimensional run stuffers with zero pass rush
Players who can only play one gap assignment
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite burst for an interior lineman — Murphy II was taken at 16 when many mocked a guard there because his explosiveness was too rare to pass up

college_production.production_floor: Multiple sacks and high pressure rate expected. Murphy II had 40 pressures including 6 sacks as a junior.
college_production.production_trajectory: Breakout junior year is acceptable — Murphy II made the leap in year 3
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Murphy II played next to T'Vondre Sweat yet still dominated, which showed he wasn't scheme-dependent

examples: 2024 1.16 — Byron Murphy II: 6-0.5, 297, 4.87 40, 32.4-inch arms. Texas DT, elite first-step quickness, 12.5% pressure rate. 2 sacks in Super Bowl LX.
2025 5.142 — Rylie Mills: Notre Dame DE/DT. Versatile interior/edge player.
2016 2.49 — Jarran Reed: 6-3, 307. Alabama DT, two-gap run stuffer who also rushed the passer.
2019 7.236 — Demarcus Christmas: 6-3, 293. Florida State DT, developmental.

CB.archetype_summary: Schneider has drafted two distinct CB archetypes that reveal his range: (1) Elite physical specimens with freakish size-speed combinations (Tariq Woolen: 6-4, 4.26 40, converted WR), and (2) Aggressive, physical cover corners who play with dog in them regardless of size (Devon Witherspoon: 5-11, taken 5th overall). The unifying thread is elite athleticism, physicality, and competitiveness. Under Macdonald's scheme, which requires both man and zone ability plus occasional blitzing, versatility is now an added requirement.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-9 to 6-4 — extremely wide range. Schneider has no height cutoff; he'll take 5-9 Tre Brown or 6-4 Tariq Woolen.
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No hard cutoff — Witherspoon had average length; Woolen had 33.6-inch arms. Values length but not at the expense of coverage ability.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed is critical. Woolen ran 4.26 (historic), Coby Bryant ran 4.46, Nehemiah Pritchett ran 4.48. Ball skills and physicality matter more than pure 40 time for some prospects though — Witherspoon didn't run at the combine but was taken 5th overall on film alone.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster strongly preferred. No CB drafted in top-5 rounds has been slower than 4.50.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Multiple — Macdonald's scheme requires both man and zone. Witherspoon excels in press-man and is used as a blitzer. Woolen plays more off-zone coverage. The scheme demands versatility.
most_valued_traits: Physicality and willingness to tackle/hit
Competitive fire and aggression
Ball skills and ability to create turnovers
Versatility in man and zone coverages
Ability to play outside and in the slot
least_important_traits: Ideal size — Schneider has proven he will take any size CB if the ability is there
deal_breakers: Passive corners who avoid contact
One-scheme-only corners who can't play both man and zone
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Freakish athletic profiles — Woolen's 4.26 at 6-4 made him a 5th-round steal. Schneider loves combine standouts at CB.

college_production.production_floor: No strict interception threshold — values film over stats at CB. Woolen had limited production as a converted WR.
college_production.production_trajectory: Willing to bet on late-blooming CBs — Woolen converted from WR to CB late. But also takes proven producers (Witherspoon, Coby Bryant).
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high — Woolen had minimal CB experience but freakish measurables. Schneider trusts development.

examples: 2023 1.5 — Devon Witherspoon: 5-11, 181. Illinois CB, consensus All-American. Elite press-man technician, aggressive tackler. Pro Bowl in each of first 2 seasons.
2022 5.153 — Tariq Woolen: 6-4, 205, 4.26 40, 33.6-inch arms. UTSA converted WR-to-CB. Pro Bowl as rookie with 6 INTs.
2022 4.109 — Coby Bryant: 6-1, 193, 4.46 40. Cincinnati CB, Jim Thorpe Award winner. Production-first pick.
2024 5.136 — Nehemiah Pritchett: Auburn CB. Physical corner.
2024 6.192 — D.J. James: Auburn CB. Athletic coverage corner.
2021 4.137 — Tre Brown: 5-10, 185, 4.32 40. Oklahoma CB. Undersized speedster.

S.archetype_summary: Large, physical, versatile safety who can play multiple roles — box safety, single-high, slot defender, and occasional blitzer. Schneider's safety archetypes draw directly from the Legion of Boom DNA (Earl Thomas, Kam Chancellor). The 2025 selection of Nick Emmanwori (6-3, 220, 4.38 40) explicitly channels Chancellor — Schneider himself noted the physical resemblance. Under Macdonald, safeties must be scheme-versatile: able to play deep zone, cover tight ends, come downhill against the run, and disguise pre-snap looks.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum, but values length — Emmanwori had 32.5-inch arms
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed is critical — Emmanwori ran 4.38 at 220 lbs, which was a historic combine performance (43-inch vertical, 11-foot-6 broad jump). Earl Thomas ran 4.46 at 195 lbs. Range and explosion are non-negotiable.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster strongly preferred for starting safeties

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Hybrid — Macdonald's defense requires safeties to play multiple roles within the same game. Emmanwori lined up as an edge rusher on one play and dropped into coverage the next during training camp.
most_valued_traits: Elite range and sideline-to-sideline speed
Physicality in run support and tackling
Versatility to play box, deep, and slot
Ball skills and turnover creation
Size to match up with tight ends in coverage
least_important_traits: Pure free safety skills only — Schneider wants do-it-all safeties, not single-role players
deal_breakers: Liability in run support or tackling
One-dimensional safeties who can only play deep
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Freakish size-speed-explosion combination — Schneider traded up 17 spots to get Emmanwori at 35, comparing him to Kam Chancellor

college_production.production_floor: No strict statistical floor — but multi-year starting experience is preferred. Thomas, Chancellor, and Emmanwori were all multi-year starters.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values early production — Emmanwori was a 3-year starter with 244 tackles and 6 INTs
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — values film and measurables over raw stats

examples: 2025 2.35 — Nick Emmanwori: 6-3, 220, 4.38 40, 43-inch vert, 32.5-inch arms. South Carolina S. Historic combine. Schneider compared to Kam Chancellor. DROY finalist.
2010 1.14 — Earl Thomas: 5-10, 202, 4.46 40. Texas S, three-time first-team All-Pro. Rangy center-field safety.
2010 5.133 — Kam Chancellor: 6-3, 225, 4.62 40. Virginia Tech SS. Physical enforcer.
2019 2.47 — Marquise Blair: 6-2, 195, 4.48 40. Utah SS. Physical hitter, Kam Chancellor comp.

LB.archetype_summary: Schneider's LB history reveals two eras: the Pete Carroll era valued sideline-to-sideline athletes who could cover (Bobby Wagner, K.J. Wright), and the Macdonald era adds emphasis on tackling production and run-stuffing. The common thread is high-volume tacklers with instincts. Tyrice Knight (2024, Rd 4) led the nation in solo tackles. Jordyn Brooks (2020, Rd 1) was also a tackling machine. Wagner was the gold standard — elite processing, sideline-to-sideline range, coverage ability.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed matters but isn't an eliminator — Wagner ran 4.55, Brooks ran 4.55, Knight ran 4.63. Processing speed and instincts trump timed speed for Schneider at this position.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-4.70 preferred but not a hard cutoff

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Tackling volume and defensive processing. Wagner's elite NFL career validated Schneider's preference for LBs who diagnose and close.
most_valued_traits: Elite tackling ability and pursuit angles
Sideline-to-sideline range
Instincts and play recognition
Coverage ability vs. TEs and RBs
Durability and ability to play every down
least_important_traits: Pass rush/blitz ability — nice to have but not a primary LB evaluation trait for Schneider
deal_breakers: Poor tacklers despite athletic testing
LBs who are liabilities in coverage against modern passing attacks
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite production (tackle totals) combined with instincts — Brooks at 27th overall was seen as a reach but justified by his tackling machine profile

college_production.production_floor: High — Wagner had 453 career tackles, Brooks led Texas Tech in tackles, Knight had 388 tackles since 2020 (tied 3rd in FBS). Schneider loves productive LBs.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — production from any conference is valued

examples: 2012 2.47 — Bobby Wagner: 6-0, 241, 4.55 40. Utah State LB. Potential Hall of Famer. Elite processor and tackler.
2020 1.27 — Jordyn Brooks: 6-0, 240, 4.55 40. Texas Tech LB. Tackling machine, 108 tackles as senior.
2024 4.118 — Tyrice Knight: 6-2, 234, 4.63 40. UTEP LB. Led nation with 84 solo tackles. Physical run defender.
2019 3.88 — Cody Barton: 6-1, 237. Utah LB. Coverage-capable, versatile player.

WR.archetype_summary: Schneider has drafted more WRs than any other position (16+) and targets two primary archetypes: (1) Physical freaks with elite size-speed combinations who win with athleticism (DK Metcalf: 6-3, 229, 4.33 40), and (2) Polished route technicians who win with precision and hands (Jaxon Smith-Njigba: 6-0, 197, the most polished route runner in the 2023 class). He is willing to bet on raw athletic upside at WR more than any other position, as evidenced by multiple developmental Day 3 WR picks.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-9 to 6-4 — extremely wide range. Takes all sizes.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed is highly valued but not absolute — Metcalf's 4.33 at 229 was legendary, but Smith-Njigba ran 4.52 and was taken 20th overall. Values explosion and play speed on film over pure combine numbers.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No hard cutoff — willing to take route technicians (JSN) and deep threats (Metcalf)

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Depends on archetype — Metcalf wins with speed and physicality, JSN wins with route running and separation. The constant is competitive blocking — Pete Carroll valued blocking WRs and Schneider still does.
most_valued_traits: Competitive toughness and willingness to block
Ability to create separation through speed or routes
Contested catch ability for bigger receivers
Return ability and special teams value (Lockett, Eskridge)
Football character and maturity
least_important_traits: Pure route-tree diversity as a prospect — Schneider will develop routes if the athletic traits are there (Metcalf was raw coming out)
deal_breakers: Passivity and lack of competitive fire
WRs who won't block in the run game — Carroll and now Macdonald demand it
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Historic combine performances — Metcalf's combine made him a must-have. Schneider is a known combine-performance drafter at WR.

college_production.production_floor: Surprisingly low — Metcalf had just 26 catches in 7 games (injury-shortened final year). Schneider trusts film and traits over production at WR when the upside is exceptional.
college_production.production_trajectory: Will draft WRs with limited production if the traits are rare. But also drafts productive WRs (JSN: 100+ catches at OSU, Tate: 2-year producer at Notre Dame, Lockett: prolific at K-State).
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — adjusts for offense, injury, and competition level

examples: 2019 2.64 — DK Metcalf: 6-3, 229, 4.33 40, 40.5-inch vert. Ole Miss WR. Historic combine. Physical freak, limited college production due to injury.
2023 1.20 — Jaxon Smith-Njigba: 6-0, 197, 4.52 40. Ohio State WR. Elite route runner, 100+ catches as sophomore. Polished technician archetype.
2015 3.69 — Tyler Lockett: 5-10, 182, 4.40 40. Kansas State WR. Return specialist and precise route runner.
2010 2.60 — Golden Tate: 5-10, 199, 4.42 40. Notre Dame WR. Physical, YAC-focused, excellent blocker.
2025 5.166 — Tory Horton: 6-1, 200. Colorado State WR. 88 catches, 1,483 yards (16.9 avg) as a junior. Productive mid-round pick.
2021 2.56 — D'Wayne Eskridge: 5-9, 190, 4.38 40. Western Michigan WR. Speed/return specialist — bust.

TE.archetype_summary: Schneider historically deprioritized TE in the draft (no pick above round 4 until 2025), preferring to acquire TEs via trade (Jimmy Graham) or free agency (Greg Olsen, Noah Fant). The 2025 selection of Elijah Arroyo at pick 50 signals an evolution. Arroyo is a receiving-first TE with enough blocking ability to be an in-line player. Under Macdonald, the TE must be a passing-game weapon who can also hold up as a blocker — hybrid profile. AJ Barner (2024, Rd 4) was described as a blocking TE first.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Values athleticism for the position — receiving TEs need speed and route running. Blocking TEs need functional strength. Arroyo (6-4, ~250) represents the athletic hybrid.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Versatility — must contribute in both pass and run game. Under Macdonald's influence (and new OC Brian Fleury from SF), the TE position is becoming more central to the offense.
most_valued_traits: Receiving ability and hands
Blocking competency
Versatility to play inline, flex, and move
Red zone target ability
Football intelligence
least_important_traits: Pure blocking-only TEs — those are acquired as veterans, not drafted early
deal_breakers: Cannot catch or run routes at all — defeats the purpose of investing draft capital
Injury history at the position
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Athletic profiles that suggest receiving upside — Arroyo (Rd 2) and Barner (Rd 4) both had significant receiving production

college_production.production_floor: Limited data — only 3 TEs drafted in top-4 rounds. Arroyo had strong receiving numbers at Miami.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values production for TEs drafted in top-4 rounds
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — adjusts for TE usage in college offense

examples: 2025 2.50 — Elijah Arroyo: 6-4, ~250. Miami TE. Receiving-first hybrid with 35 catches, 590 yards, 7 TDs as senior.
2024 4.121 — AJ Barner: 6-4, 242. Michigan TE. Reliable receiver and blocker.
2025 5.175 — Robbie Ouzts: 6-2, ~250. Alabama FB/TE. First FB drafted by Seattle since 2014. H-back type.

RB.archetype_summary: Schneider values physical, downhill runners who can also contribute in the passing game. Kenneth Walker III (2022, Rd 2) is the archetype — an explosive, powerful runner with home-run ability and decent pass-catching skills. The regime prefers acquiring starting-caliber RBs in round 2-3, not round 1. They also value depth and committee approaches — frequently drafting complementary backs (Zach Charbonnet, Rashaad Penny, Chris Carson via UDFA). Physical toughness and ability to absorb punishment are non-negotiable.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Burst and explosion — vertical jump and broad jump. Walker ran 4.38 with a 36-inch vertical. Speed matters but vision and power are equally important.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-4.55 preferred for early-round RBs. Walker ran 4.38. Charbonnet ran 4.53.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Decisive, physical running between the tackles with enough burst to break long runs. Walker's 89-yard TD run in the Super Bowl embodied the archetype.
most_valued_traits: Explosive burst through the hole
Physical running style and contact balance
Breakaway speed for home-run plays
Receiving ability out of the backfield
Pass protection willingness
least_important_traits: Elite pass-catching ability — nice to have but not required. Physical running comes first.
deal_breakers: Lack of physicality between the tackles
Liability in pass protection
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Home-run speed combined with physical running — Walker's combine (4.38, 36-inch vert) at 211 lbs was electric

college_production.production_floor: High — Walker had 1,636 yards and 18 TDs at Michigan State. Charbonnet had 1,357 yards and 14 TDs at UCLA. Schneider wants proven producers.
college_production.production_trajectory: Transfer portal success is valued — both Walker (Wake Forest to Michigan State) and Charbonnet (Michigan to UCLA) thrived after transferring
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — production in any conference is valued

examples: 2022 2.41 — Kenneth Walker III: 5-9, 211, 4.38 40, 36-inch vert. Michigan State RB, 1,636 yards, 18 TDs. Super Bowl MVP (2025).
2023 2.52 — Zach Charbonnet: 6-1, 214, 4.53 40. UCLA RB, 1,357 yards, 14 TDs. Physical complementary back.
2018 1.27 — Rashaad Penny: 5-11, 220, 4.46 40. San Diego State RB. Explosive home-run hitter.
2025 7.223 — Damien Martinez: Miami/Oregon State RB. 4.51 40. Pac-12 Freshman of the Year 2022.

QB.archetype_summary: Schneider has only drafted three QBs in 16 drafts: Russell Wilson (Rd 3, 2012), Alex McGough (Rd 7, 2018), and Jalen Milroe (Rd 3, 2025). This extreme scarcity makes QB archetype analysis low-confidence. Wilson was a dual-threat with elite processing and deep-ball accuracy. Milroe is a dual-threat with elite rushing ability and developing passing. The common thread is mobility and playmaking ability beyond the pocket, combined with arm talent. Schneider has historically preferred acquiring QBs via trade/FA (Geno Smith trade, Sam Darnold FA) rather than committing high draft capital. He publicly stated the 2025 QB class was 'deeper than advertised' and used a 3rd-rounder on Milroe behind Darnold, suggesting a developmental approach.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-11 to 6-3 — Wilson was 5-11, Milroe is 6-1. No height premium.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Mobility is clearly valued — both Wilson and Milroe are dual-threats. Arm talent matters (Milroe had 23 TDs, 6 INTs as Alabama starter). But pure running ability is tolerated more than most assume — Milroe led Alabama with 20 rushing TDs in 2024.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Mobility valued but not strict 40 time — it's about play-extending ability and rushing upside

playing_style.primary_style: Dual-threat QBs who can beat you with both arm and legs. Not pure pocket passers — both Wilson and Milroe are dynamic runners who extend plays.
most_valued_traits: Arm talent and ability to make all the throws
Mobility and play-extending ability
Competitive fire and clutch performance
Leadership and intangible qualities
Processing speed and decision-making under pressure
least_important_traits: Prototype size — Schneider has shown zero height bias by drafting Wilson at 5-11
deal_breakers: Limited arm talent
Poor decision-making under pressure
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: N/A — too few data points. But Wilson's 3rd-round selection was itself a bet on a unique player.

college_production.production_floor: Must be a proven starter. Wilson started multiple years at NC State and Wisconsin. Milroe started 2 years at Alabama.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values winners — Wilson won 33 of 37 starts in college. Milroe won a Manning Award finalist nod.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high — adjusts for talent around QB and conference

examples: 2012 3.75 — Russell Wilson: 5-11, 206, 4.55 40. Wisconsin/NC State QB. 33-1 record as starter. Elite processor, accurate deep ball, dual-threat. Won Super Bowl XLVIII.
2025 3.92 — Jalen Milroe: 6-1, ~215. Alabama QB. 23 TDs, 6 INTs passing; 20 rushing TDs as senior. Campbell Trophy winner. Developmental pick behind Darnold.
2018 7.220 — Alex McGough: FIU QB. Developmental flier.

QB.confidence_note: Only 3 QBs drafted across 16 drafts. Schneider's QB evaluation is more apparent in trade/FA acquisitions (Wilson retention, Smith trade, Darnold signing) than draft picks.

reach_tolerance.notes: Schneider has a documented history of 'surprising' picks that analysts viewed as reaches. L.J. Collier at 29 in 2019 was widely seen as a 2nd-round talent. Jordyn Brooks at 27 in 2020 was viewed as a reach by many. Marquise Blair at 47 in 2019 was considered overdrafted. However, his recent top-10/top-15 picks (Cross at 9, Witherspoon at 5, Murphy at 16, Zabel at 18) have been in-line with or better than consensus. The pattern is: the later in the 1st round Schneider picks, the more likely he reaches. When picking in the top-20, he's more disciplined.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Outside-zone run scheme under new OC Brian Fleury (hired from 49ers). Values athletic, mobile linemen who can pull and reach-block. Passing game built around play-action and quick-game concepts off zone-run action. Previously ran Klint Kubiak's offense in 2024-2025 (also zone-based).
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Multiple front defense under Mike Macdonald (Ravens coaching tree). Primarily 4-down nickel personnel (87% nickel usage, leading NFL in 2025). Generates pressure through four-man rushes with interior stunts and edge games, NOT blitzing (one of lowest blitz rates in NFL). Demands versatile defenders who can play multiple roles and disguise coverages. Both man and zone coverage used. Front four dominance is non-negotiable — DT and EDGE must be disruptive without extra help.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: Macdonald hired January 2024. The defensive scheme transition happened in 2024 (Year 1 growing pains, dominant second half). By 2025, it was fully installed — Seattle won Super Bowl LX with the #1-ranked run defense and top-5 overall defense. OC changed from Kubiak to Fleury after SB LX, but both run outside-zone concepts so minimal disruption expected.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderate-to-aggressive. Schneider takes calculated risks. He drafted Tariq Woolen (converted WR with minimal CB experience) in round 5 — huge upside bet that paid off. He traded Russell Wilson when others would have held on. He traded DK Metcalf and Geno Smith when they wanted out rather than overpaying. But he also takes 'safe' picks like Cross (proven pass protector) and Zabel (Senior Bowl dominant). His Day 3 picks tend to be higher-risk athletic projects.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate. After terrible run defense in 2022-2023, he took DT Byron Murphy II at 16 in 2024. After IOL being the clear weakness in 2024, he took Zabel at 18 in 2025. But he won't force a need pick if the value isn't there — he took Murphy over a guard when the DT value was clearly superior.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Moves on quickly from busts. Cut Jamal Adams after 4 injury-riddled seasons despite giving up 2 first-round picks. Traded DK Metcalf rather than overpaying. Released Tyler Lockett for cap reasons. Does not double-down on past investments if they're not working.
psychological_profile.combine_description: High combine weight — one of the most notable combine-influenced drafters in the NFL. DK Metcalf's historic combine made him a must-pick. Tariq Woolen's 4.26 at 6-4 made him a 5th-round steal. Nick Emmanwori's historic combine (4.38 at 220 lbs, 43-inch vert) prompted a trade-up. Seattle selected a league-high 4 players with athleticism scores of 85+ in 2025. Schneider clearly uses combine data to identify athletic outliers at premium positions.
psychological_profile.character_description: High character emphasis. The team's culture page emphasizes 'competitive guys that are about football, about the process, smart, tough and reliable.' Schneider's own quotes emphasize looking at 'the whole person.' Grey Zabel turned down significant NIL offers to stay at NDSU — character trait Schneider specifically valued. Macdonald has moved away from Carroll's tolerance for individualism toward team-first culture.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Extremely conservative QB drafter. Only 3 QBs drafted in 16 years. Prefers to acquire starting QBs via trade (Wilson retention) or free agency (Darnold at $100.5M). Will draft a developmental QB in rounds 3-7 behind an established starter (Milroe behind Darnold in 2025) but will not use round 1-2 capital on a QB. Has never traded up for a QB. The only scenario for early QB draft investment would be a franchise-altering prospect falling in a year where Seattle lacks a long-term answer.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Balanced but tilts toward projection when the athletic profile is historic. Schneider will take a less productive player with elite traits (Metcalf with 26 catches, Woolen as a converted WR) over a more productive player with average traits. However, at most positions, he wants proven multi-year starters with real production (Wagner, Brooks, Walker, Cross, Witherspoon all had strong college production). The production-vs-projection split varies sharply by position: at WR and CB, he'll bet on traits; at LB and OL, he wants production.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: No minimum — Metcalf had 26 catches in shortened final year. Traits override production here.
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: 1,000+ yards preferred for Day 2 picks. Walker had 1,636; Charbonnet had 1,357; Penny had 2,248.
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: 5+ sacks in final season for Day 2 picks. Mafe had 7, Hall had 8.5, Taylor had 8.5.
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No clear statistical threshold — trusts film over interception totals. Woolen had minimal CB stats.
production_thresholds_by_position.LB: High tackle totals valued — Wagner 453 career, Brooks 108 as senior, Knight 388 over 4 years.
production_thresholds_by_position.OL: Multi-year starting experience is the threshold, not statistical production.

college_production_preferences.notes: Schneider is NOT a breakout-age drafter. He has taken late bloomers (Woolen converting from WR as a redshirt junior) and early producers (Wagner starting as a sophomore). What matters more than breakout age is the athletic profile and competitive traits. The combine is where Schneider identifies the players whose athletic profiles make them worth the projection risk.

CB.notes: No CB drafted in top-5 rounds slower than 4.50. Speed is critical.

EDGE.notes: Collier's 4.91 was the exception and the biggest bust. Mafe 4.53, Hall 4.51, Taylor 4.57.

WR.notes: Ranges from 4.33 (Metcalf) to 4.52 (JSN). Values play speed over combine speed but loves freakish testers.

OT.notes: Cross 34.25, Lucas 33.5, Okung 34.75. Long arms are non-negotiable at tackle.

S.notes: Emmanwori 4.38, Thomas 4.46, Blair 4.48. Speed and range are essential.

DT.notes: Murphy II 4.87 at 297 lbs. Burst matters for 3-technique types.

IOL.notes: Values athletic testing (Zabel's 36.5 vert) but no strict 40 cutoff.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Mostly soft preferences with occasional hard cutoffs at premium positions (CB speed, OT arm length). Schneider's testing thresholds function more as 'must-hit to trigger interest' than 'miss-and-you're-eliminated.' The exception is OT arm length, which appears to be a genuine hard cutoff.

size_preferences_by_position.DT: 297-320 range for 3-technique. Two-gap nose can be heavier (Jarran Reed 307+).
size_preferences_by_position.LB: 225-255, no strong size preference — has drafted from 237 (Barton) to 241 (Wagner) to 240 (Brooks)
size_preferences_by_position.S: Prefers 6'0"+ safeties with range. Emmanwori at 6-3/220 is the new prototype.
size_preferences_by_position.CB: No size preference whatsoever. Takes any size from 5-9 (Tre Brown) to 6-4 (Woolen).
size_preferences_by_position.WR: No size preference. Takes any profile from 5-9 (Eskridge) to 6-3/229 (Metcalf).

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: Vertical jump is weighted heavily as an explosiveness proxy across multiple positions (Zabel 36.5, Metcalf 40.5, Emmanwori 43.0, Walker 36.0). 40-yard dash is critical at CB, S, WR, and EDGE. Schneider is one of the most combine-influenced GMs in the league — a league-high 4 players with 85+ athleticism scores in 2025.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Schneider uses measurables as an amplifier, not a replacement for film. When a player has both elite film AND elite testing, Schneider is aggressive (Witherspoon at 5, Murphy at 16, Metcalf trade-up). When testing contradicts film, film wins for experienced players (Witherspoon didn't run at the combine, taken 5th) but testing wins for raw prospects (Woolen's 4.26 overrode limited CB experience). The synthesis: Schneider is a film-first evaluator who uses measurables to identify upside that film alone might not fully capture.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Premium positions with the highest-rated player on his board, with a slight lean toward addressable need. Has stayed put at his pick in rounds 1 for recent drafts (2020-2025), reversing the 2013-2019 trend of trading down. When picking in the top-20, takes safe, high-floor, high-ceiling players (Cross, Witherspoon, Murphy, Zabel). Rarely swings for pure upside in round 1 — saves that for later rounds.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: This is where Schneider does his best work. Round 2-3 is where he finds franchise-altering players: Russell Wilson (Rd 3), Bobby Wagner (Rd 2), Frank Clark (Rd 2), DK Metcalf (Rd 2), Boye Mafe (Rd 2), Kenneth Walker (Rd 2), Nick Emmanwori (Rd 2), Derick Hall (Rd 2). He shifts toward need-filling here but still demands athletic upside. This is also where he's most active in trading — will trade up for a specific target (Metcalf, Lockett, Emmanwori) or trade down to accumulate more Day 2 picks.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Athletic development projects and role players. Schneider uses Day 3 to take fliers on exceptional athletes with limited college production (Woolen in Rd 5, converted WR-to-CB) and special teams contributors. He also targets OL depth, backup QBs, and positional redundancy. Has taken at least one 'traits over production' pick on Day 3 in nearly every draft. This is where he mines small schools (UTSA for Woolen, D-II Findlay for Jerrell, NDSU for Zabel) and converts position-changers.
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: Historically didn't play the comp pick game (only 1 comp pick in 7 years before 2024). Since hiring Macdonald (Ravens influence), Seattle has begun accumulating comp picks. In 2025, had three comp picks (Rds 5, 5, 7). Uses comp picks for similar purposes as regular picks — not noticeably higher-risk.

EDGE.note: Strong evaluation. Irvin, Clark, Mafe, Hall all became starters. Collier and Taylor were misses.

CB.note: Excellent when hits land — Witherspoon (Pro Bowl), Woolen (Pro Bowl), Sherman (HoF-caliber UDFA). But many late-round CBs haven't panned out.

WR.note: Mixed — Metcalf, JSN, Lockett, Tate are smashes. But Eskridge, Jennings, and many Day 3 WRs were busts. Volume compensates for misses.

OL.note: Cross (franchise LT), Okung, Lucas, Zabel (expected starter). But Ifedi, Pocic, and several interior picks disappointed.

DT.note: Murphy II (dominant), Jarran Reed (solid starter), Mebane (holdover). Collier cross-listed as DE/DT miss.

LB.note: Wagner (potential HoF), Brooks (solid starter), Knight (emerging). Excellent evaluation.

S.note: Thomas (HoF), Chancellor (HoF-caliber), Emmanwori (DROY finalist). Historically elite.

RB.note: Walker (SB MVP), Penny (flash but injury-prone), Charbonnet (quality RB2).

QB.note: Wilson was a 3rd-round pick who won a Super Bowl — one of the greatest draft steals ever. Milroe TBD.

TE.note: Arroyo (2025 rookie, TBD), Barner (role player), Sullivan (bust). Historically weak position in the draft.

blind_spots: Late-round WR evaluation has been poor — most Day 3 WR picks have been busts
TE evaluation is virtually non-existent due to lack of draft investment — rely on trade/FA
IOL evaluation was historically weak (Ifedi, Pocic) but 2024-2025 picks (Haynes, Zabel) suggest improvement
strengths: S evaluation is historically elite — Thomas, Chancellor, Emmanwori
LB evaluation is above-average — Wagner, Brooks, Knight
Finding athletic outliers at CB in mid-to-late rounds — Woolen and Sherman (UDFA) are both legendary finds
EDGE evaluation in round 2 has been consistently strong — Clark, Mafe, Hall

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Strategic, targeted spending. Fills 1-2 high-priority holes with veteran starters (DeMarcus Lawrence, Sam Darnold, Cooper Kupp in 2025; Leonard Williams re-signing in 2024), then uses the draft for the remaining needs. Not a top spender but willing to invest significant dollars when the fit is right. Recently adopted a comp-pick-conscious approach, signing players whose deals won't offset comp picks (Laken Tomlinson).
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When a veteran starter is signed at a position, that position drops in draft priority but isn't completely abandoned. Example: signed Lawrence at EDGE in 2025 but still drafted Rylie Mills (DE) in round 5 for depth. Signed Darnold at QB but still drafted Milroe in round 3 as developmental backup. Schneider believes in building positional depth through every avenue.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build foundational talent through the draft at premium positions (EDGE, CB, OT, DT, S), supplement through FA at positions where veterans can bridge gaps (QB, LB, IOL), and use trade market aggressively for both acquisition and divestiture. Schneider has completed over 3,900 roster transactions as GM — the most prolific transaction-maker in the modern NFL. His philosophy is: never stop turning over the bottom of the roster, protect the core, and be willing to make bold moves (trading Wilson, trading Metcalf) when the long-term math demands it.

notable_patterns: Has traded down in round 1 in 6 of 16 drafts, but has stayed put in the last 6 years when picking in the top-20
Has taken at least one defensive player in round 1 or 2 in every single draft
16+ WRs drafted — more than any other position — reflecting Schneider's belief in taking multiple shots
Only 3 QBs drafted in 16 years — most extreme QB avoidance in the draft among long-tenured GMs
2022 draft was the best in franchise history and one of the best single-year draft classes in NFL history (Cross, Mafe, Walker, Lucas, Bryant, Woolen)
League-high 4 players with 85+ athleticism scores drafted in 2025 — Schneider is an elite combine evaluator
Every first-round pick since 2022 has been a productive starter or better (Cross, Witherspoon, JSN, Murphy, Zabel)

biggest_reaches.context: Post-Frank Clark trade, needed a DE. Collier's 4.91 40 was a red flag. Biggest bust of Schneider's tenure.
LB wasn't seen as a premium-round need. Wagner and Wright were still on the roster. Viewed as a reach at the time but Brooks became a solid starter.

biggest_steals.context: Converted WR with 4.26 speed at 6-4. Made Pro Bowl as rookie with 6 INTs. One of the biggest steals in modern draft history.
Third-round QB who won Super Bowl XLVIII. Arguably the greatest draft steal in Seahawks history.
Fell due to injury concerns and poor 3-cone time despite historic combine. Schneider traded up to get him.

confidence_notes: 16 drafts of data as Seattle GM make this the highest-confidence profile possible. Schneider is the longest-tenured GM in the NFL and his patterns are well-established across multiple roster cycles (Legion of Boom era, post-Wilson rebuild, Macdonald era). The only uncertainty is how the Macdonald hire continues to shift defensive player archetypes — the first two Macdonald-era drafts (2024-2025) already show clear Ravens-influenced preferences (interior DL premium, versatile defensive players, comp pick strategy). Profile should be refreshed after 2026 draft to capture continued Macdonald-era evolution.
